Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd have these bursts where I'd study for hours, especially when something visual caught my attention like diagrams or video content, but then I'd go days without touching anything. I know I logged about 31 hours a week, but it wasn't organized at all. I'd study late at night mostly, whenever the anxiety kicked in about falling behind, then I'd crash and ignore everything for a bit.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, man. Deadlines were basically my alarm clock for studying. I'd coast along feeling pretty chill, then suddenly realize something was due and scramble. Sometimes my extracurriculars would take over and studying just got pushed aside. The only consistent thing was my inconsistency, if that makes sense.
Q3: Usually it was panic, to be real with you. I'd remember an assignment was coming up or I'd get a notification and think 'oh no, what did I miss now?' Sometimes I logged in just to check if there was anything urgent, scrolled around a bit, then logged off. Occasionally I was genuinely curious about new content, but that was rare.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more frequently, maybe after a professor mentioned something in class, but it never stuck. My attendance was pretty low anyway, so I wasn't getting those regular reminders. It always felt like something I had to force myself to do rather than something natural.
Q5: The video content was definitely most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I could watch lectures, pause, rewind, and actually see concepts demonstrated. Some of the diagrams and infographics in the course materials really helped things click. Discussion posts were okay too because seeing how other students understood things gave me different perspectives I wouldn't have thought of myself.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading-heavy PDFs and long text documents. My eyes would just glaze over, you know? I'd download them with good intentions but never actually read them properly. The discussion forums I only used minimally because typing out my thoughts felt like a chore. If it wasn't visual or interactive, I probably skipped it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it directly connected to something I'd be assessed on. Visual content that explained complex ideas quickly was gold. If I could finish something and actually feel like I understood it better, that was worth it. But if it felt like busy work or just clicking through slides, I'd lose interest fast.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. If it wasn't required, my brain filed it under 'ignore.' Required activities had that weight of consequences behind them, so even when I procrastinated, I knew I'd eventually have to do them. Optional activities felt like suggestions I could decline, and I always declined.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of videos and materials to watch offline on my own time. I'd screenshot diagrams and study from my phone while commuting or just lying in bed. I also used YouTube a lot to find alternative explanations of concepts. My notes were scattered across different apps and notebooks that the LMS would never know about.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be studying hard but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd have downloaded a lecture video and watched it three times over a weekend, but the system only registered one download. Or I'd be working through problems in a notebook, referencing screenshots I'd taken weeks ago. My real studying happened offline more than online.
Q11: All the time, yeah. I'd access something once, download it, and then that was my study material for days or weeks. The videos especially - I'd save them and rewatch them before exams. The LMS would show I only clicked on it once, but I probably engaged with that content ten times more than that shows.
Q12: My anxiety would spike sometimes and everything felt overwhelming even though my stress was generally low. When that happened, I'd just avoid the course entirely for a few days. Also when my extracurriculars got intense, the course became background noise. There were weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to care about logging in.
Q13: Definitely. When I had competing responsibilities from my other activities, the LMS was the first thing to get neglected. Fatigue was real too - after a long day, the last thing I wanted was to stare at more screens. Confusion made me avoid things too; if I didn't understand something, I'd rather just not deal with it than ask for help.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have the LMS open while scrolling my phone or watching something else. I'd click through videos without really absorbing anything, just to feel like I was doing something productive. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph five times because my mind was elsewhere. The activity was there, but my brain wasn't.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even though I knew they mattered. The anxiety would tell me it was too much, so I'd avoid it to feel better in the moment. Then the deadline would approach and I'd panic-work through it. It's a terrible cycle but somehow I kept doing it. Important didn't mean urgent in my brain until it actually was urgent.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without a deadline, I wouldn't touch the LMS. As deadlines approached, my login frequency would spike dramatically. I'd cram everything into the last possible window, submit, then disappear again until the next deadline appeared. It wasn't healthy but it worked for my grades.
Q17: Feedback did affect me when I actually received it. Good feedback made me feel like maybe I knew what I was doing, which was nice. Negative feedback sometimes made me avoid that area of the course for a while because of the anxiety. But honestly, I didn't always check feedback right away - sometimes I'd wait days before looking at it.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which was both good and bad for someone like me. Good because I could work at my own pace, bad because I had no external structure forcing me to stay consistent. If there had been more mandatory check-ins or weekly requirements, I probably would have engaged more regularly instead of in bursts.
Q19: The visual content made engagement easier for sure. The video lectures were well-produced and held my attention. What made it harder was when there was too much text-based content or when the navigation felt confusing. Also, having six online courses meant the LMS was overwhelming - too many tabs, too many notifications, too much everything.
Q20: I think that's people like me who are browsing, downloading, maybe watching stuff, but then struggling to actually complete and submit work. High anxiety can make you engage with content repeatedly without feeling confident enough to submit anything. You're trying to learn but the final step of turning something in feels too scary or the work isn't quite good enough yet.
Q21: That's literally me though. My exam score was over 90 and I got a Distinction, but my LMS engagement was low. I studied a lot offline - downloaded materials, external resources, my own notes. The LMS doesn't see me lying in bed reviewing flashcards I made or watching YouTube explanations. Performance and logged activity aren't the same thing.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked, how long pages were open. But they can't show if you were actually paying attention or just had it open in another tab. They miss offline studying completely. They can't measure understanding or real engagement - just surface-level activity. It's like measuring how many times someone opened the fridge, not whether they actually ate.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not about how many times you click or how long you're logged in. For me, engagement happens when I'm genuinely curious and focused, even if that's happening offline with downloaded materials. It's about the quality of attention, not the quantity of clicks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my anxiety levels or how that affected my engagement patterns. It wouldn't show the mindfulness exercises I did to manage that anxiety before studying. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded content or external videos. And it definitely wouldn't show that despite looking disengaged online, I was actually learning effectively in my own way.
